extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Thermal incubator system consisting of an insulated styrofoam box, a heatbed, a fan, two internal temperature sensors and one external temperature sensor, all controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The system’s goal is to maintain a target temperature regardless of its contents, making it a representative cyber‑physical system with measurable thermal dynamics and controllable actuators suitable for digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Real‑time monitoring and visualization of temperature and actuator states via a time‑series database and dashboard; on‑demand calibration of thermal models (Model A with two free parameters and Model B with four free parameters) using a tracking simulator; anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation through a Kalman filter; what‑if simulation and controller optimisation via co‑simulation of historical or live data; a closed self‑adaptation loop that detects changes in plant behaviour, schedules experiments, recalibrates Model B, reconfigures the Kalman filter and controller, and optimises control parameters.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Hardware: Raspberry Pi, heatbed, fan, styrofoam enclosure, two internal and one external temperature sensors. Software stack: RabbitMQ message broker for communication between low‑level driver and controller; low‑level driver abstracts sensor/actuator I/O; bang‑bang controller on the Pi with parameters LL, UL, H, C; Kalman filter for state estimation; calibration process (tracking simulator) and co‑simulation framework for what‑if analysis; time‑series database for storing telemetry; dashboard for visualization; simulation models (Model A and Model B) used for prediction and optimisation. Enablers: data‑driven calibration, model‑at‑runtime, and a digital twin framework that listens to RabbitMQ messages.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The DT system is orchestrated using an event‑driven architecture centered around RabbitMQ (AMQP) as the message broker. The physical twin (incubator) is instrumented with a low‑level driver that periodically reads temperature sensors and publishes data to RabbitMQ. The controller (running on a Raspberry Pi) consumes temperature data, executes a bang‑bang control strategy, and publishes actuator commands back to RabbitMQ. Digital‑twin components subscribe to the same topics: a calibration service that runs model‑parameter estimation (Model A/B) whenever new data arrives; a monitoring service that checks safety limits and triggers alerts; a Kalman‑filter‑based anomaly detector that estimates the unmeasured heat‑bed temperature; a what‑if simulation engine that performs co‑simulations using the latest model parameters; and a self‑adaptation loop that schedules experiments, re‑calibrates, and re‑configures the controller. All data is stored in a time‑series database (e.g., InfluxDB) and visualised on a dashboard. Resource management is handled by the RabbitMQ broker and the underlying OS; the DT components are loosely coupled, enabling horizontal scaling and fault isolation.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The system integrates with external components via RabbitMQ using the AMQP protocol for intra‑system messaging. Sensor data and actuator commands are exchanged through the low‑level driver and controller over this broker. The DT also writes processed data to a time‑series database for visualization, but no other external enterprise systems or interoperability standards are explicitly referenced in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Temperature data is collected by two sensors inside the incubator and one sensor outside. The low‑level driver running on the Raspberry Pi polls these sensors at a periodic interval (exact frequency not specified in the documents) and publishes the readings to the RabbitMQ message broker. The data payload consists of floating‑point temperature values (in degrees Celsius) encapsulated in a lightweight message format (e.g., JSON or plain text). The driver also includes a safe protocol that validates sensor readings before forwarding them. The data is then consumed by the digital twin components (calibration algorithms, monitors) via RabbitMQ subscriptions, enabling real‑time visualization and model updates.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control messages from the Digital Twin are sent via RabbitMQ to the Raspberry Pi controller, which implements a bang‑bang control strategy. The controller issues commands to the fan and heatbed through the low‑level driver, following a safe protocol. The low‑level driver executes the actuator commands and publishes the resulting PT state back to RabbitMQ for confirmation and feedback.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DT and PT communicate over a local network using RabbitMQ as the message broker. RabbitMQ is deployed on premises (or in the cloud) and provides reliable, persistent message delivery. The network topology is a star where the Raspberry Pi and DT components connect to the RabbitMQ broker. No explicit bandwidth or latency requirements are specified, but the system relies on low‑latency message passing to support real‑time control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The Physical Twin runs on a Raspberry Pi that hosts the low‑level driver and controller. RabbitMQ is deployed on the same local network (either on the Pi or a separate on‑premise machine). extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle in the incubator case study follows a sequence of phases: (1) Physical Twin (PT) definition – hardware (styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, sensors, Raspberry Pi) and software (controller, low‑level driver, RabbitMQ communication server) are specified and deployed; (2) Model development – two thermal models (Model A with two free parameters, Model B with four free parameters) are derived from first‑principles and simplified assumptions; (3) Calibration – the models are calibrated against experimental data using an automated calibration routine (Section 3.3) that adjusts the free parameters to minimize prediction error; (4) Simulation & validation – calibrated models are run in co‑simulation with the PT, generating predictions that are compared to measured temperatures; (5) Deployment – the calibrated models are embedded in the DT framework, where they are accessed by a Kalman‑filter‑based state estimator, an anomaly‑detector, and a what‑if simulation engine; (6) Monitoring & optimization – real‑time data from the PT are ingested via RabbitMQ into a time‑series database, visualised on a dashboard, and used by the Kalman filter to estimate heat‑bed temperature and detect deviations; (7) Self‑adaptation – when an anomaly is detected, the DT triggers an experiment, re‑configures the controller, re‑calibrates Model B, re‑runs what‑if simulations to optimise controller parameters, and finally re‑deploys the updated controller; (8) Governance – all artifacts (hardware schematics, source code, model definitions, calibration scripts) are version‑controlled in a public GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO‑CPS‑Association/example‑incubator) and documented in the open‑source project, ensuring traceability and reproducibility.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process follows an open‑source, model‑at‑runtime approach: 1) **Requirements & Design** – The system is defined by a target temperature range and a bang‑bang control strategy (Docs 6, 3). 2) **Model Creation** – Differential‑equation based simulation models (Model A and Model B) are implemented and validated against measured data (Doc 3). 3) **Calibration & Validation** – Automatic calibration (section 3.3) adjusts model parameters; validation is performed by comparing simulation outputs to sensor readings (Doc 5). 4) **Version Control** – All source code, model definitions, and configuration files are stored in a GitHub repository, enabling traceability and collaborative development (Docs 2, 5). 5) **Testing & QA** – While explicit unit or integration tests are not documented, the calibration routine and simulation comparisons serve as functional validation. 6) **Evolution Management** – New experiments (e.g., introducing a cold water bucket) trigger re‑calibration, model update, and controller re‑configuration, ensuring the DT evolves with the PT (Doc 5).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The incubator Digital Twin provides decision‑support through several analytics layers: (1) Real‑time visualization – sensor streams (two inside‑box, one outside) are ingested via RabbitMQ into a time‑series database (e.g., InfluxDB) and plotted on a dashboard (Grafana) that displays temperature, heater and fan status, and controller state; (2) Anomaly detection – a Kalman‑filter‑based state estimator uses Model B to estimate the unmeasured heat‑bed temperature; deviations between estimated and measured temperatures trigger anomaly flags; (3) Heat‑bed state estimation – the Kalman filter outputs a probabilistic estimate of the heat‑bed temperature with associated covariance, providing confidence bounds for controller decisions; (4) What‑if simulation – the DT runs co‑simulations that replay historical or live PT data, allowing the user to vary controller parameters (LL, UL, H, C) and observe predicted temperature trajectories; (5) Self‑adaptation loop – upon anomaly detection, the DT schedules an experiment, re‑configures the controller, re‑calibrates Model B, re‑optimises controller parameters via simulation, and finally updates the controller, thereby closing the loop; (6) Confidence measures – the Kalman filter’s covariance matrix and the RMSE of the calibrated models are reported alongside predictions, giving users quantitative confidence in the recommendations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
- **Simulation models**: Two thermal models of the incubator – Model A (2 free parameters) and Model B (4 free parameters). Model B captures the heat‑bed inertia and is preferred for further DT development.  
- **Data sources**: Temperature readings from two sensors inside the styrofoam box and one sensor outside the box.  
- **Data management**: Sensor data is uploaded to the RabbitMQ communication server and stored in a time‑series database for real‑time dashboards and for feeding the Kalman filter and co‑simulation engines.  
- **Model relationships**: Calibration algorithms (offline or on‑demand) adjust the free parameters of Model B based on collected data; the calibrated model is then used by the Kalman filter for state estimation and by what‑if simulation modules for controller optimisation.  
- **Roles in the DT**: Models provide predictive behaviour of the physical twin; data feeds monitoring, anomaly detection, and optimisation algorithms; algorithms (calibration, Kalman filter, co‑simulation) close the loop between the physical and digital twins.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
- **Actuators**: Heat‑bed (electric heater) and fan (air circulation).  
- **Control mechanism**: Bang‑bang controller implemented on a Raspberry Pi.  
- **Control parameters**: Lower limit (LL) and upper limit (UL) for temperature, heating duration (H), and waiting duration (C).  
- **Remote control interface**: Commands and state updates are exchanged via RabbitMQ messages between the controller and the low‑level driver.  
- **Actuation ranges & limitations**: Heating is toggled on/off; the duration of heating (H) and cooling wait (C) are configurable but bounded by the controller logic to avoid overheating.  
- **Safety constraints**: A safe protocol is embedded in the low‑level driver to prevent unsafe states; the controller waits after each actuation to ensure temperature does not exceed UL.  
- **Addressing mechanisms**: Each actuator is addressed through the RabbitMQ topic hierarchy (e.g., `incubator/actuator/heatbed`), allowing decoupled communication.

Sensing infrastructure: three temperature sensors are installed – two inside the styrofoam box (one near the heatbed, one near the fan) and one outside the box. The sensors provide real‑time temperature readings.

extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
Model fidelity: Model B, with four free parameters, captures the heatbed’s thermal inertia and provides higher fidelity predictions than Model A.
Validation and verification: the calibration algorithm (section 3.3) adjusts the model parameters to fit measured temperature data; the resulting model is validated by comparing simulation outputs to real‑time sensor data.
Uncertainty: a Kalman filter is employed to estimate the unmeasured heatbed temperature and to detect anomalies; the filter’s covariance matrix quantifies estimation uncertainty.
Error detection and correction: anomalies detected by the Kalman filter trigger the self‑adaptation loop, which schedules a new experiment, re‑calibrates Model B, and updates the Kalman filter parameters.

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

**2.2.1** **The Communication Server**


For the case study, the RabbitMQ [4] server was deployed for synchronisation and communication. With tens of thousands of users, RabbitMQ is one of the most popular
open source message brokers. RabbitMQ is a mature, lightweight, and easy to deploy
on premises and in the cloud.

The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands of controlling the fan and the heatbed and at the same time sharing the control
states to the RabbitMQ server.
We choose RabbitMQ because it greatly facilitates the digital twinning process, as
the DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed
between the controller and the load level driver, as illustrated in fig. 7.


**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the
controller. The controller parameters are:

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].

how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.
